Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - CONVERGENCE OF GENERALIZED BELIEF PROPAGA TION ALGORITHM ON GRAPHS WITH MOTIFS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Belief propagation is a fundamental message-passing algorithm for numerous applications in machine learning. It is known that belief propagation algorithm is exact on tree graphs. However, belief propagation is run on loopy graphs in most applications. So, understanding the behavior of belief propagation on loopy graphs has been a major topic for researchers in different areas. In this paper, we study the convergence behavior of generalized belief propagation algorithm on graphs with motifs (triangles, loops, etc.) We show under a certain initialization, generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Undirected graphical models, also known as Markov Random Fields (MRF), provide a framework for modeling high dimensional distributions with dependent variables. Ising models are a special class of discrete pairwise graphical models originated from statistical physics. Ising models have numerous applications in computer vision Ravikumar et al. (2010), bio-informatics Marbach et al. (2012), and social networks Eagle et al. (2009). Explicitly, the joint distribution of Ising model is given by

where _{Xi}i ∈{±_ 1 _}[n]_ are random variables valued in a binary alphabet (also known as "spins"), _Jij_ represents the pairwise interactions between spin _i_ and spin _j_ , _hi_ represents the external field for spin _i_ , _β_ = 1 _/T_ is the reciprocal temperature, and _Z_ is a normalization constant called _partition function_ .

Historically, Ising models are proposed to study ferromagnetism. However, researchers find the computational complexity is the main challenge of performing sampling and inference on Ising models. In the literature, there are multiple ways to tackle the computational complexity. One of the ways are Markov-Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms. A well-known example is Gibbs sampling, which is a special case of the Metropolis–Hastings algorithm. Basically Gibbs sampling samples a random variable conditioned on the distribution based on the previous samples. It can be shown that Gibbs sampling generates a reversible Markov chain of samples. Thus, the stationary distribution of the Markov chain is the desired joint distribution over the random variables, and it can be reached after the _burn-in period_ . However, it is also well-known that Gibbs sampling will become trapped on multi-modal distribution. For example, Smith and Roberts (1993) and Mengersen (1996) show that when the joint distribution is bi-modal, the Gibbs sampling iterations may be trapped in one of the modes, reducing the probability of convergence.

Another popular way to go around the computational complexity is _variational methods_ , which makes some approximation to the joint distribution. These methods usually turn the inference problem with respect to the approximate joint distribution into some non-convex optimization problem, and solve it either by the standard optimization methods, e.g, gradient descent, or by specialized algorithms like belief propagation. However, due to the non-convexity, those methods usually do not have theoretical guarantees that the solution converges to the global optimum.

Belief propagation (BP) is an effective numerical method for solving inference problems on graphical models. It was originally proposed by Pearl (2014) for tree-like graphs. Ever since it plays a fundamental role in numerous applications including coding theory Frey et al. (1998); Richardson

and Urbanke (2001), constraint satisfaction problems Achlioptas and Moore (2006), and community detection in the stochastic block model Decelle et al. (2011). It is well-known that belief propagation is only exact for a model on a graph with locally tree-like structures. The long haunting question is, theoretically how does belief propagation perform on loopy graphs.

We now describe the related work and our contributions.

## **Related work and our contribution**

In a classic work, Yedidia et al. (2003) establishes the connection between belief propagation and the Bethe free energy. He shows that there is one-to-one correspondence between the fixed points of belief propagation and stationary points of the Bethe free energy. Following his work, it is known that the Bethe free energy at the critical points can be represented in terms of fixed point messages of belief propagation Montanari (2013). In a recent work, Koehler (2019) further studies the properties of Bethe free energy at the critical points, and shows for ferromagnetic Ising models, initialized with all one messages, belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy. However, those theories consider either asymptotic locally tree-like graphs, or loopy graphs with simple edges. Real technological, social and biological networks have numerous short and large loops and other complex motifs, which lead to non-tree-like structures and essentially loopy graphs with hyper edges. Newman Newman (2009); Karrer and Newman (2010) and Miller (2009) independently propose a model of random graphs with arbitrary distributions of motifs. And Yoon et al. (2011) generalizes the Belief Propagation to graphs with motifs.

Our work builds on generalized belief propagation on graphs with motifs Yoon et al. (2011) and the convergence of belief propagation on ferromagnetic Ising models on loopy graphs with simple edges Koehler (2019). In this paper, we show for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, with all messages initialized to one, generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy.

## 2 ISING MODELS ON GRAPHS WITH MOTIFS

Let us introduce the concept of graphs with motifs. In graphs with motifs, each vertex belongs to a given set of motifs. As shown in Fig.1a , different motifs can be attached to vertex _i_ : a simple edge ( _i, j_ ), a triangle, a square, a pentagon, and other non-clique motifs. Graphs with motifs can be viewed as hyper-graphs where motifs play a role of hyper-edges. And the number of specific motifs attached to a vertex is equal to hyper-degree with respect to the specific motifs. In this paper, for simplicity, we only consider simple motifs such as simple edges, and cliques.

Consider the Ising model with arbitrary order of interactions among vertices in each motif on a hyper-graph. Let _Ml_ ( _i_ ) denote a cluster of size _l_ attached to vertex _i_ , where vertices _j_ 1 _, j_ 2 _, . . . , jl−_ 1 together with _i_ form the motif. And let _X_ denote the random variable of spin configurations, the Hamiltonian of the model is

where the first sum corresponds to the external fields at each vertex, the second sum corresponds to the pairwise interactions on simple edges, the third sum corresponds to the higher order interactions among spins in triangles, the fourth sum corresponds to the higher order interactions among spins in squares, and so on. As discussed in the previous section, most previous works focus on Ising models with pairwise interactions. In this paper, we are interested in Ising models with higher order interactions. For simplicity, we consider Ising models with only external fields and higher order interactions in triangles. Our derivation can be extended to more general cases.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a genuinely interesting and non-trivial problem: extending convergence guarantees for belief propagation from loopy graphs with simple edges to graphs with motifs (e.g., triangles), building naturally on Koehler (2019) and Yoon et al. (2011). The motivation is clear and the positioning relative to prior work is adequate. However, several significant concerns arise from the opening sections. First, the contribution appears quite incremental—essentially combining two existing frameworks—and the introduction does not clearly articulate what technical novelty or difficulty is involved in this extension beyond mechanical generalization; the authors should explicitly highlight which proof steps required new ideas versus direct adaptation. Second, the scope is narrower than the title suggests: the analysis is restricted to ferromagnetic Ising models with only external fields and triangle interactions, yet this restriction is introduced almost in passing with a vague claim that "our derivation can be extended to more general cases," which needs justification rather than assertion. Third, the writing suffers from several clarity and presentation issues—key equations referenced in the text appear to be missing or improperly rendered, there are minor grammatical problems ("one of the ways are"), and the abstract/introduction do not precisely state the initialization condition or the assumptions under which convergence holds. Finally, the paper would benefit substantially from a concrete motivating application or experimental validation mentioned upfront, as well as a clearer statement of the main theorem in the introduction so readers can assess the contribution without reading deeply into the technical sections.

comments.title: Lemma 2 hypothesis uses pairwise form but claim is about motif messages
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered whether this was just an OCR artifact since the paper is about triangle motifs. Reading carefully, the function f here has the form tanh(h + Σ tanh^{-1}(x_i x_j)) — a product of TWO variables per term — but the BP consistency equation (12) for triangle motifs should involve products of the form tanh^{-1}(θ_{ijk} ν_{j→ik} ν_{k→ij}) or similar (three-body terms with θ_{ijk}). The Lemma as stated corresponds to the pairwise Koehler (2019) setting, not to the triangle-motif generalization this paper claims to extend. Since Lemma 2 is the key ingredient used to prove monotonicity of φ on [x*,1)^n and hence the main Theorem 1, using a function that does not match the actual φ for triangle motifs is a substantive gap. Even if one argues that after a change of variable x_i x_j stands in for a product of messages along a motif, the θ_{ijk} (which was just defined before the Corollary) does not appear, and the number of arguments per tanh^{-1} term does not match the motif structure. This seriously weakens the proof of the main result.

comments.title: Gradient proof of Lemma 3 is essentially empty
comments.quote:
_Proof._ The lemma will follow if we compute the gradient of the dual Bethe free energy function _G[∗]_ Bethe[(] _[ν]_[)][.]

Recall _φ_ ( _ν_ ) _i→jk_ is the updated message from spin _i_ to motif _{j, k}_ based on the current messages _ν_ . If _ν ∈ S_ pre or _S_ post, then the signs of the gradient of Bethe free energy are determined by Equation (66) as claimed.
comments.explanation: The claim ∇G*_Bethe(ν) ≤ 0 on S_pre and ≥ 0 on S_post is central to the proof of Theorem 1 (it is what makes the line-integral argument work). The proof as given says essentially 'compute the gradient, and by equation (66) the signs are as claimed', without actually exhibiting the gradient or explaining why it factors into a sign determined by φ(ν) − ν (or whatever defines S_pre/S_post). Without seeing the gradient computation, a reader cannot verify this key step, and the final argument (integrating ∇G_Bethe(ν)·(ν* − ν) along the segment) relies on this sign property. This is a significant justification gap for one of the two main technical lemmas.

comments.title: Dual vs. primal Bethe free energy conflated in final line integral
comments.quote: By Lemma 3, we see that for any point _x_ on this interpolating line segment that _∇G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _·_ ( _ν[∗] − ν_ ) _≥_ 0, and integrating over this line segment gives us _G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _≤ G_ Bethe( _ν[∗]_ ).
comments.explanation: Lemma 3 is stated in terms of the dual Bethe free energy G*_Bethe(ν), which is the natural object parameterized by messages ν (indeed the paper explicitly introduces the dual so that it is a function of ν). But the concluding inequality in the proof of Theorem 1 uses G_Bethe, the primal Bethe free energy on the belief polytope. The proof writes ∇G_Bethe(ν) — but G_Bethe is a function of beliefs P, not of messages ν, so this is formally undefined unless the authors tacitly identify G_Bethe(ν) with G*_Bethe(ν) via the critical-point parametrization. Even granting that identification, the final claim G_Bethe(ν) ≤ G_Bethe(ν*) purports to compare Bethe free energy values at arbitrary ν ∈ S_post with that at the fixed point ν*, but stationarity of G_Bethe holds only at BP fixed points, while intermediate ν on the line segment are not fixed points. The authors likely mean G*_Bethe throughout; the notation switch obscures whether the argument is legitimate and whether the conclusion (global maximum of Bethe free energy, as advertised in the abstract) really follows.

comments.title: Existence of a fixed point in [x*,1)^n is assumed, not established
comments.quote: _Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi_[(0)] _→jk_[=][1] _[ and there is at] least one fixed point in_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] .
comments.explanation: The main theorem is conditional on the existence of at least one BP fixed point in the region [x*,1)^n where monotonicity holds. The abstract and introduction claim that 'generalized BP converges to the fixed point at the global maximum of the Bethe free energy' for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, without qualification. The existence of such a fixed point is not proved in the text shown, and x* itself is defined as max_{(i,j)} x*_{ij} where x*_{ij} is 'the largest root of g(x) in (0,1)' — so x* depends on the graph. The reader is left to assume that a fixed point exists in this region, but for ferromagnetic models starting from all ones and φ monotonically decreasing, one needs to ensure the limit does not drop below x*. This is a real gap between the advertised claim and what is proved.

comments.title: Maximization vs minimization of Bethe free energy is unconventional

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a genuinely interesting and non-trivial problem: extending convergence guarantees for belief propagation from loopy graphs with simple edges to graphs with motifs (e.g., triangles), building naturally on Koehler (2019) and Yoon et al. (2011). The motivation is clear and the positioning relative to prior work is adequate. However, several significant concerns arise from the opening sections. First, the contribution appears quite incremental—essentially combining two existing frameworks—and the introduction does not clearly articulate what technical novelty or difficulty is involved in this extension beyond mechanical generalization; the authors should explicitly highlight which proof steps required new ideas versus direct adaptation. Second, the scope is narrower than the title suggests: the analysis is restricted to ferromagnetic Ising models with only external fields and triangle interactions, yet this restriction is introduced almost in passing with a vague claim that "our derivation can be extended to more general cases," which needs justification rather than assertion. Third, the writing suffers from several clarity and presentation issues—key equations referenced in the text appear to be missing or improperly rendered, there are minor grammatical problems ("one of the ways are"), and the abstract/introduction do not precisely state the initialization condition or the assumptions under which convergence holds. Finally, the paper would benefit substantially from a concrete motivating application or experimental validation mentioned upfront, as well as a clearer statement of the main theorem in the introduction so readers can assess the contribution without reading deeply into the technical sections.

comments.title: Double definition of λ'_{i→jk} with identical formula for two different derivatives
comments.quote: Setting _λ[′] i→jk_[=] _[λ][i][→][j][k]_[(][1][)] _[−]_ 2 _[λ][i][→][j][k]_[(] _[−]_[1][)] , we find that at a critical point of the Lagrangian that
comments.explanation: This exact definition of λ'_{i→jk} appears twice — once after the ∂L/∂P_{ijk} equation and once after the ∂L/∂P_i equation. On its face this is redundant but not wrong: if λ'_{i→jk} is a single reparameterization used in both derivations, stating it twice is merely repetitive. However, the two critical-point equations derived (from ∂L/∂P_{ijk} and ∂L/∂P_i) should in general produce different relations between the original λ multipliers and the beliefs, and it's unusual that the same reparameterization works cleanly for both. Without the actual equations shown (OCR has stripped them), a reader cannot verify that the reparameterization is consistent across both derivatives. If the two derivations genuinely produce the same λ' definition, the repetition is harmless; if different combinations were intended, one of them is a transcription/OCR issue that obscures the derivation.

comments.title: Lemma 2 hypothesis uses pairwise form but claim is about motif messages
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered whether this was just an OCR artifact since the paper is about triangle motifs. Reading carefully, the function f here has the form tanh(h + Σ tanh^{-1}(x_i x_j)) — a product of TWO variables per term — but the BP consistency equation (12) for triangle motifs should involve products of the form tanh^{-1}(θ_{ijk} ν_{j→ik} ν_{k→ij}) or similar (three-body terms with θ_{ijk}). The Lemma as stated corresponds to the pairwise Koehler (2019) setting, not to the triangle-motif generalization this paper claims to extend. Since Lemma 2 is the key ingredient used to prove monotonicity of φ on [x*,1)^n and hence the main Theorem 1, using a function that does not match the actual φ for triangle motifs is a substantive gap. Even if one argues that after a change of variable x_i x_j stands in for a product of messages along a motif, the θ_{ijk} (which was just defined before the Corollary) does not appear, and the number of arguments per tanh^{-1} term does not match the motif structure. This seriously weakens the proof of the main result.

comments.title: Gradient proof of Lemma 3 is essentially empty
comments.quote:
_Proof._ The lemma will follow if we compute the gradient of the dual Bethe free energy function _G[∗]_ Bethe[(] _[ν]_[)][.]

Recall _φ_ ( _ν_ ) _i→jk_ is the updated message from spin _i_ to motif _{j, k}_ based on the current messages _ν_ . If _ν ∈ S_ pre or _S_ post, then the signs of the gradient of Bethe free energy are determined by Equation (66) as claimed.
comments.explanation: The claim ∇G*_Bethe(ν) ≤ 0 on S_pre and ≥ 0 on S_post is central to the proof of Theorem 1 (it is what makes the line-integral argument work). The proof as given says essentially 'compute the gradient, and by equation (66) the signs are as claimed', without actually exhibiting the gradient or explaining why it factors into a sign determined by φ(ν) − ν (or whatever defines S_pre/S_post). Without seeing the gradient computation, a reader cannot verify this key step, and the final argument (integrating ∇G_Bethe(ν)·(ν* − ν) along the segment) relies on this sign property. This is a significant justification gap for one of the two main technical lemmas.

comments.title: Dual vs. primal Bethe free energy conflated in final line integral
comments.quote: By Lemma 3, we see that for any point _x_ on this interpolating line segment that _∇G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _·_ ( _ν[∗] − ν_ ) _≥_ 0, and integrating over this line segment gives us _G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _≤ G_ Bethe( _ν[∗]_ ).
comments.explanation: Lemma 3 is stated in terms of the dual Bethe free energy G*_Bethe(ν), which is the natural object parameterized by messages ν (indeed the paper explicitly introduces the dual so that it is a function of ν). But the concluding inequality in the proof of Theorem 1 uses G_Bethe, the primal Bethe free energy on the belief polytope. The proof writes ∇G_Bethe(ν) — but G_Bethe is a function of beliefs P, not of messages ν, so this is formally undefined unless the authors tacitly identify G_Bethe(ν) with G*_Bethe(ν) via the critical-point parametrization. Even granting that identification, the final claim G_Bethe(ν) ≤ G_Bethe(ν*) purports to compare Bethe free energy values at arbitrary ν ∈ S_post with that at the fixed point ν*, but stationarity of G_Bethe holds only at BP fixed points, while intermediate ν on the line segment are not fixed points. The authors likely mean G*_Bethe throughout; the notation switch obscures whether the argument is legitimate and whether the conclusion (global maximum of Bethe free energy, as advertised in the abstract) really follows.

comments.title: Existence of a fixed point in [x*,1)^n is assumed, not established

The abstract and introduction claim that 'generalized BP converges to the fixed point at the global maximum of the Bethe free energy' for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, without qualification. The existence of such a fixed point is not proved in the text shown, and x* itself is defined as max_{(i,j)} x*_{ij} where x*_{ij} is 'the largest root of g(x) in (0,1)' — so x* depends on the graph. The reader is left to assume that a fixed point exists in this region, but for ferromagnetic models starting from all ones and φ monotonically decreasing, one needs to ensure the limit does not drop below x*. This is a real gap between the advertised claim and what is proved.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and potentially valuable problem—extending convergence guarantees for belief propagation from pairwise ferromagnetic Ising models to generalized belief propagation on graphs with motifs—but the presentation in the opening sections is not yet at a sufficiently rigorous or polished level for a strong conference submission. The main contribution is stated clearly at a high level, but the technical setting is underspecified: it is unclear exactly what class of motifs, higher-order interactions, ferromagnetic conditions, external fields, and region/Bethe free-energy approximations are covered, and the paper alternates between “global optimum” and “global maximum” without clarifying the objective’s sign convention. Definitions of graphs with motifs, hyperedges, triangle interactions, and generalized BP messages need to be formalized much more carefully before the convergence theorem can be evaluated. The related-work discussion is relevant but somewhat shallow and imprecise, with several awkward or inaccurate phrasings and citations; it should more clearly distinguish the proposed result from Koehler (2019), Yedidia et al., and prior generalized BP work. The writing also contains many grammatical issues, formatting problems, and missing or poorly rendered equations, which significantly reduce clarity. Most importantly, the paper’s central claim appears quite strong—global convergence of GBP on loopy motif-based hypergraphs—so the assumptions, proof strategy, and relation to the nonconvexity of the generalized Bethe/Kikuchi free energy must be stated with much greater precision. Overall, the topic is promising, but the current exposition raises concerns about rigor, scope, and clarity of the claimed theoretical contribution.

comments.title: Abstract and contribution overstate the motif generality proved later
comments.quote: In this paper, we study the convergence behavior of generalized belief propagation algorithm on graphs with motifs (triangles, loops, etc.)
comments.explanation: The abstract reads as a claim about generalized BP on broad motif or hyperedge structures, including loops, squares, cliques, and possibly non-clique motifs. Later, however, the formal setup is narrowed to external fields and higher-order interactions in triangles, with messages such as \(\nu_{i\to jk}\) written specifically for vertex-to-triangle interactions. The authors acknowledge this simplification, but the abstract and contribution statement still present the result as applying to graphs with motifs broadly. A statement that the derivation can be extended is not the same as proving convergence for loops, squares, general cliques, or arbitrary motif distributions.

comments.title: Main convergence and global-optimality claims exceed the restricted theorem and proof
comments.quote: We show under a certain initialization, generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs.
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly frames the result as convergence to a global Bethe optimum, but the formal theorem is more limited. It assumes all-one initialization and the existence of a fixed point in \([x^*,1)^n\), while the proof shows convergence to the coordinatewise largest fixed point only within the domain where monotonicity is established. The theorem also states dominance over any other fixed point, although the proof only establishes comparison for fixed points in \([x^*,1)^n\). The final optimality argument compares points in a post-fixpoint set rather than the full Bethe polytope or all message configurations, and it additionally needs an unproved coordinatewise relation \(\nu^*\ge \nu\) for arbitrary post-fixpoints to justify the gradient dot-product inequality. Thus the proof does not establish the global optimum claim as stated.

comments.title: The term “ferromagnetic” is used in a nonstandard way for this higher-order model
comments.quote: Definition 1. An Ising model is ferromagnetic if Jijk ≥ 0 for all triangle motifs ( i, j, k ) and hi ≥ 0 for all i.
comments.explanation: The paper explicitly defines its convention, but the terminology can still mislead. In standard pairwise Ising models, “ferromagnetic” usually refers to nonnegative couplings, while external fields may have arbitrary signs. Here the theorem also requires nonnegative fields. Moreover, for a three-spin interaction \(J_{ijk}X_iX_jX_k\), positive \(J_{ijk}\) does not have the same attractive/all-spins-aligning meaning as a positive pairwise coupling; it favors configurations with product \(+1\), including configurations with two negative spins. The result should be described as requiring nonnegative higher-order product coefficients and nonnegative fields, rather than relying on the usual ferromagnetic intuition.

comments.title: MCMC convergence discussion conflates stationary convergence with finite burn-in and mixing
comments.quote: Thus, the stationary distribution of the Markov chain is the desired joint distribution over the random variables, and it can be reached after the _burn-in period_ . However, it is also well-known that Gibbs sampling will become trapped on multi-modal distribution.
comments.explanation: The motivational point that MCMC can mix slowly on multimodal distributions is valid, but the wording is technically too strong. For an irreducible, aperiodic Gibbs chain, the target distribution is approached asymptotically; it is not literally reached after a finite burn-in period except approximately. Similarly, multimodality usually causes metastability or very slow mixing, not failure of eventual convergence for a valid ergodic chain.

comments.title: Notation \(M_l(i)\) is ambiguous when multiple motifs of the same size touch a vertex
comments.quote: Let _Ml_ ( _i_ ) denote a cluster of size _l_ attached to vertex _i_ , where vertices _j_ 1 _, j_ 2 _, . . . , jl−_ 1 together with _i_ form the motif.
comments.explanation: As written, \(M_l(i)\) looks like a single motif determined by vertex \(i\) and size \(l\). In a graph with motifs, however, a vertex can be incident to many triangles or other motifs of the same size. The notation should either include an additional motif index or define \(M_l(i)\) as a generic element of a set such as \(\partial_l i\). Otherwise the Hamiltonian sums and message indices are ambiguous.

comments.title: GBP variable belief is described as the actual probability on loopy graphs
comments.quote: By the definition of generalized Belief Propagation, the probability that spin i is in a state Xi is determined by the normalized product of incoming intermediate messages from motifs attached to spin i and the external field factor e[βh][i][X][i] ,
comments.explanation: The product of incoming messages is the standard BP/GBP formula for a variable belief and is exact on an appropriate hypertree. On loopy motif graphs, however, this quantity is generally an approximate belief or marginal estimate, not the true marginal probability. The text should call it the GBP belief for spin \(i\), rather than “the probability,” unless exactness assumptions are being imposed.

comments.title: Exact hypertree factorization needs a more precise tree and separator condition
comments.quote: If our graph were tree-like hyper-graph with triangle motifs only (see Fig. 1b as an example), we can in fact do that. In that case, we can represent the joint probability distribution in the form
comments.explanation: The intended Bethe/hypertree factorization using triangle beliefs divided by singleton beliefs is correct only under suitable acyclicity and separator assumptions. In a motif hypergraph, two triangles could share an edge or overlap in more than one vertex, in which case singleton separators are insufficient and the factorization would require pair-region beliefs or a different junction-tree structure. The text should specify that the triangle motifs form an acyclic factor graph, or otherwise state the required intersection condition explicitly.

comments.title: BP–Bethe stationary-point correspondence needs positivity, boundary, and gauge qualifications
comments.quote: we can verify that a set of beliefs gives a BP fixed point in any hyper-graph if and only if they are stationary points of the Bethe free energy for the generalized BP.
comments.explanation: The BP–Bethe correspondence is standard, but the unqualified statement is stronger than the derivation supports. The Lagrange multiplier calculation applies to positive, locally consistent beliefs in the relative interior of the local polytope, with message normalization/gauge choices and region-graph counting numbers handled appropriately. Normalization and marginalization constraints alone do not imply nonnegativity; signed arrays can satisfy those linear constraints. Boundary optima require separate KKT or subgradient treatment. The paper should state that it is considering interior stationary points with strictly positive beliefs rather than claiming that the inequality constraints are automatically satisfied.

comments.title: The dual Bethe objective is derived only at critical points but later used off-critical in message space
comments.quote: Lemma 1. The dual Bethe free energy at a critical point can be defined by
comments.explanation: Lemma 1 derives the dual Bethe expression using stationarity conditions, which is enough to evaluate the Bethe free energy at BP fixed points. Later, however, the proof differentiates the dual objective with respect to messages and integrates its gradient along line segments of messages that are not necessarily fixed points. That requires an independently defined smooth off-critical dual objective on the relevant message domain and a justification that its values and gradients support the claimed comparison. The notation also switches between the primal Bethe free energy \(G_{\mathrm{Bethe}}(P)\) and the dual/message objective \(G^*_{\mathrm{Bethe}}(\nu)\), which obscures which function is being optimized.

comments.title: Local tilted distribution in Lemma 1 does not follow directly from the original Boltzmann law
comments.quote: From Equation (4), we know
comments.explanation: In the proof of Lemma 1, the authors introduce a local function containing the triangle interaction plus message/Lagrange-multiplier fields and then replace the local entropy-plus-energy term by a log-partition expression. This is a standard entropy-conjugacy or stationarity identity, but it does not follow directly from Equation (4), which is the full original Boltzmann distribution without the added local message fields. The proof should cite the Gibbs variational identity or the stationarity expression for \(P_{ijk}\), rather than presenting the step as a direct consequence of the original joint distribution.

comments.title: Lemma 2 does not establish concavity for the actual GBP update
comments.quote: Lemma 2. Suppose that f(x) = tanh(h + … tanh−1(xi xj)) for any h ≥ 0. Then f is a concave monotone function on the domain [x*, 1)n.
comments.explanation: The lemma appears intended to prove monotonicity and concavity of the GBP update map, but the actual update contains coupling factors such as \(\theta_{ijk}=\tanh(J_{ijk})\) inside terms of the form \(\tanh^{-1}(\theta_{ijk}\nu_m\nu_n)\). Lemma 2 omits these factors, and the Hessian and threshold \(x^*\) can depend on them. The proof also uses the claim \(x<f(x)\), which is false even for simple allowed cases: with \(h=0\) and one product term, \(f(x,x)=x^2<x\) for \(0<x<1\). Since these steps are used to justify the root defining \(x^*\) and the concavity domain, Lemma 2 as stated does not prove the property needed for the actual GBP map.

comments.title: Initialization at message value one lies outside the stated open message domain
comments.quote: Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi(0)→jk = 1 and there is at least one fixed point in [x*, 1)n.
comments.explanation: The all-one initialization is standard in ferromagnetic BP arguments, but the message parametrization \(\nu=\tanh(\lambda')\) gives \(\nu\in(-1,1)\) for finite fields, and the lemma’s domain \([x^*,1)^n\) excludes the initial value \(1\). The first update may be well-defined for finite couplings, but the paper should clarify whether the initialization is interpreted as a limit from below or whether the first iterate is guaranteed to enter the stated domain.

comments.quote: In this paper, we study the convergence behavior of generalized belief propagation algorithm on graphs with motifs (triangles, loops, etc.)
comments.explanation: At first this reads as a claim about generalized BP on fairly general motif/hyperedge structures, including loops, squares, cliques, and possibly non-clique motifs. Section 2 narrows the actual model substantially: after introducing arbitrary motifs, the paper says it considers “only external fields and higher order interactions in triangles,” and the subsequent BP messages and theorem are written specifically as messages from a vertex to a triangle pair, e.g. \(\nu_{i\to jk}\). comments.title: Claim of convergence to the global Bethe optimum is stronger than the theorem appears to establish
comments.quote: We show under a certain initialization, generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs.
comments.explanation: The intended high-level message is clear: the paper aims to extend Koehler-style monotone BP convergence guarantees to a motif setting. However, checking the later theorem and proof, the formal result appears more conditional and more local in domain. The theorem assumes initialization at all-one messages and also assumes the existence of at least one fixed point in a particular interval \([x^*,1)^n\). The proof then shows convergence to the coordinate-wise largest fixed point in that interval, and the optimality argument is made over the post-fixpoint set \(S_{post}\) via a line-segment/gradient argument. That does not by itself establish a global optimum over the full Bethe polytope, nor even necessarily over all message space or all stationary points unless additional arguments are supplied. Thus the abstract and related-work paragraph’s “global optimum/global maximum” phrasing seems to overstate what is actually justified later.

comments.title: The “ferromagnetic Ising” claim hides an extra nonstandard field restriction
comments.quote: generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs.
comments.explanation: For standard pairwise Ising models, “ferromagnetic” usually refers to nonnegative couplings \(J_{ij}\ge 0\), while the external fields may have arbitrary signs. I checked the later formal definition, and the paper defines ferromagnetic here as both \(J_{ijk}\ge 0\) and \(h_i\ge 0\). That stronger assumption is important for the monotonicity/positive-message arguments used later. The later definition resolves the authors’ intended meaning, so this is not an internal contradiction, but the abstract and introduction could mislead readers into thinking the theorem covers the standard ferromagnetic class with arbitrary external fields. The result should be described as requiring nonnegative fields as well as nonnegative interactions.

comments.title: MCMC convergence discussion conflates stationary convergence with finite burn-in and mixing
comments.quote: Thus, the stationary distribution of the Markov chain is the desired joint distribution over the random variables, and it can be reached after the _burn-in period_ . However, it is also well-known that Gibbs sampling will become trapped on multi-modal distribution.
comments.explanation: The authors are making a standard motivational point: MCMC can mix very slowly on multimodal distributions, motivating variational/message-passing methods. That point is valid. But the phrasing is technically too strong. For an irreducible, aperiodic Gibbs chain, the target distribution is approached asymptotically; it is not literally “reached” after a finite burn-in period except approximately. Similarly, multimodality typically causes metastability and exponentially slow mixing, not a failure of eventual convergence or a reduced “probability of convergence” for a valid ergodic chain. This is introductory motivation rather than a core proof step, but it is a real technical imprecision.

comments.title: Notation \(M_l(i)\) is ambiguous when multiple motifs of the same size touch a vertex
comments.quote: Let _Ml_ ( _i_ ) denote a cluster of size _l_ attached to vertex _i_ , where vertices _j_ 1 _, j_ 2 _, . . . , jl−_ 1 together with _i_ form the motif.
comments.explanation: The intended object is understandable: a motif/hyperedge of size \(l\) incident to vertex \(i\). But as written, \(M_l(i)\) looks like a single motif determined by \(i\) and \(l\). In a graph with motifs, a vertex can be incident to many triangles, many squares, etc., so \(M_l(i)\) is not uniquely defined without an additional index or an explicit convention that it denotes a generic member of a set of motifs. Later the paper uses notation such as \(\partial i\) and \(\nu_{i\to jk}\), which partly resolves the intent for triangles, but the initial definition could mislead readers when reconstructing the Hamiltonian sums or message indices. A notation like \(M\in\partial_l i\) would avoid the ambiguity.

comments.title: “Ferromagnetic” is defined in a nonstandard way for odd-order interactions
comments.quote: Definition 1. An Ising model is ferromagnetic if Jijk ≥ 0 for all triangle motifs ( i, j, k ) and hi ≥ 0 for all i.
comments.explanation: I understand why the authors impose both nonnegative triangle couplings and nonnegative fields: later monotonicity arguments for messages initialized at one appear to rely on positivity of the effective fields/messages. However, for a three-spin interaction of the form J_{ijk} X_i X_j X_k, the condition J_{ijk} ≥ 0 is not “ferromagnetic” in the usual attractive-Ising/FKG sense. A positive odd-order product favors configurations with product +1, which includes (+,+,+) but also configurations with two negative spins; it does not simply favor all spins aligning. Thus a reader familiar with ferromagnetic pairwise Ising models may incorrectly infer standard attractiveness properties that do not hold automatically here. The definition is explicit, so this is not a hidden notation error, but the paper should either justify this terminology for odd-order motifs or state clearly that it only means nonnegative higher-order product coefficients plus nonnegative fields.

comments.title: GBP variable belief is described as the actual probability on loopy graphs
comments.quote: By the definition of generalized Belief Propagation, the probability that spin i is in a state Xi is determined by the normalized product of incoming intermediate messages from motifs attached to spin i and the external field factor e[βh][i][X][i] ,
comments.explanation: The product-of-incoming-messages formula is the standard BP/GBP expression for a variable belief, and it is exact on an appropriate hypertree. I checked the surrounding context: the authors later distinguish tree-like hypergraphs from loopy hypergraphs and introduce Bethe approximations, so they probably intend this as the GBP belief rather than the true marginal probability. Still, as written, “the probability” is too strong for the loopy motif graphs that are the paper’s main target. On loopy graphs this quantity is an approximate belief produced by GBP, not generally the true marginal probability. Rephrasing as “the GBP belief/marginal estimate for spin i” would avoid a misleading interpretation.

comments.title: Exact hypertree factorization needs a more precise tree/separator condition
comments.quote: If our graph were tree-like hyper-graph with triangle motifs only (see Fig. 1b as an example), we can in fact do that. In that case, we can represent the joint probability distribution in the form
comments.explanation: The intended formula is presumably the usual Bethe/hypertree factorization using triangle beliefs divided by singleton beliefs raised to q_i−1. That is correct for an acyclic factor graph whose factor intersections are represented through variable nodes. However, “tree-like hyper-graph with triangle motifs” is somewhat ambiguous in a motif setting: two triangle motifs could share an edge, or more generally overlap in more than one vertex, in which case singleton separators are not sufficient and the stated factorization would require pair-region beliefs or a different junction-tree/separator structure. The figure may intend the simpler case where triangles meet only through individual vertices, so the concern may be resolved by that intended model, but the text should specify the required acyclicity/intersection condition rather than relying on the informal phrase “tree-like hyper-graph.”

comments.title: Ignoring nonnegativity constraints is not justified by the equality constraints alone
comments.quote: where we ignore the constraints Pi ( xi ) , Pijk ( xi, xj, xk ) ≥ 0 because, given other constraints, those constraints are always satisfied at a critical point.
comments.explanation: I see what the authors are trying to do: the standard BP–Bethe stationary-point derivation is usually carried out in the relative interior of the local polytope, where beliefs are strictly positive and entropy derivatives are well-defined. For positive graphical-model potentials, the physically relevant BP beliefs are indeed positive. But the explanation given here is mathematically inaccurate. Normalization and marginalization constraints do not by themselves imply nonnegativity; signed arrays can satisfy all of those linear equalities. What is true is that the entropy terms restrict the differentiable domain to positive beliefs, and boundary optima require separate KKT/subgradient treatment. The paper should say that it is considering interior stationary points with strictly positive beliefs, rather than claiming the inequality constraints are automatically satisfied from the other constraints.

comments.title: BP–Bethe stationary point correspondence is stated without needed interior/gauge qualifications
comments.quote: we can verify that a set of beliefs gives a BP fixed point in any hyper-graph if and only if they are stationary points of the Bethe free energy for the generalized BP.
comments.explanation: This is a standard and important result in the Yedidia–Freeman–Weiss framework, and the authors are right to use it as motivation for deriving the BP equations from Bethe first-order conditions. However, the unqualified “if and only if” is stronger than what the displayed derivation supports. The usual correspondence is between positive, locally consistent beliefs in the interior of the local polytope and BP fixed points, modulo message normalization/gauge choices and subject to the particular region graph/counting numbers being used. Boundary points of the Bethe optimization problem are not covered by the simple Lagrange multiplier stationarity equations, and generalized BP region graphs can introduce additional consistency/counting-number subtleties. This can be fixed by adding the standard qualifications, but as stated it overstates the derivation.

comments.title: The dual Bethe free energy is introduced only at critical points but later treated as an off-critical objective
comments.quote: Lemma 1. The dual Bethe free energy at a critical point can be defined by
comments.explanation: The phrase “at a critical point” is important: the preceding derivation expresses beliefs in terms of Lagrange multipliers/messages using stationarity conditions. That is enough to evaluate the Bethe free energy at BP fixed points. However, the later optimization-landscape argument differentiates the dual Bethe free energy with respect to messages and integrates its gradient along line segments of messages that are not necessarily fixed points. That requires an independently defined smooth extension of the dual objective to an open message domain, together with an argument that its values and gradients are the right ones for comparing Bethe stationary points/global optima. The current passage does not supply that bridge; deriving a formula only “at a critical point” is not sufficient to justify off-critical gradient-sign arguments.

comments.title: Local tilted distribution in Lemma 1 does not follow directly from the original Boltzmann law
comments.quote: From Equation (4), we know
comments.explanation: In the proof of Lemma 1, the authors introduce a local function f(X)=J_{ijk}X_iX_jX_k plus message/Lagrange-multiplier fields and then a local normalization constant Z_{ijk}. The desired identity is a standard entropy-conjugacy/log-partition identity, and it can also be motivated by the stationarity equations for P_{ijk}. But it does not follow directly from Equation (4), which is the full joint Boltzmann distribution of the original model without the added λ′ fields. The intended step is likely correct if rephrased as “by the Gibbs variational identity” or “by the stationarity expression for P_{ijk},” but citing the original joint distribution is misleading and skips the actual justification for replacing the local entropy-plus-energy term by log Z_{ijk}.

comments.title: Concavity lemma does not match the actual GBP update because the coupling factors are omitted
comments.quote: **Lemma 2.** _Suppose that f_ ( _x_ ) = tanh( _h_ +[] ( _i,j_ )[tanh] _[−]_[1][(] _[x][i][x][j]_[))] _[for][any][h][≥]_[0] _[.][Then][f][is][a] concave monotone function on the domain_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] ._
comments.explanation: I first read this as an abstract version of the GBP update map, and the surrounding context supports that intent: immediately before this section the paper defines \(\theta_{ijk}=\tanh(J_{ijk})\), and earlier the fixed-point update has terms of the form \(\tanh^{-1}(\tanh(J_{imn})\nu_{m\to in}\nu_{n\to im})\). Thus the map whose monotonicity/concavity is needed is closer to \(\tanh(h+\sum \tanh^{-1}(\theta_{ij}x_i x_j))\), with nonnegative but generally non-unit \(\theta_{ij}\). The lemma as stated drops these \(\theta\)-factors. This is not just a cosmetic omission: the Hessian and any lower-threshold \(x^*\) for concavity depend quantitatively on the \(\theta\)'s, and one cannot generally absorb arbitrary motif-dependent \(\theta_{ijk}\) into globally shared message variables without changing the map. The intended result may well be true with extra factors, but as written Lemma 2 does not directly establish the property needed for the actual GBP map \(\phi\).

comments.title: Lemma 2 proof uses a false inequality for the stated function
comments.quote: Due to the fact _x < f_ ( _x_ ), we know _g_ ( _x_ ) _→∞_ as _x →_ 0, and _g_ (1) _<_ 0.
comments.explanation: I tried to interpret this charitably as referring to the scalar restriction of the function in Lemma 2, perhaps with all coordinates set equal. But for the lemma as stated, \(x<f(x)\) is not generally true under the stated assumption \(h\ge 0\). For example, with \(h=0\) and a single product term, \(f(x,x)=\tanh(\tanh^{-1}(x^2))=x^2<x\) for \(0<x<1\). Including the actual coupling factor \(\theta\le 1\) would only make this comparison weaker in many cases. Since this inequality is used to infer the behavior of \(g\) near zero and to justify the existence/location of the root defining \(x^*\), the concavity proof appears to rely on an assumption that is not stated and is false in simple allowed cases.

comments.title: The theorem’s dominance claim is broader than what the proof establishes
comments.quote: **Theorem 1.** _Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi_[(0)] _→jk_[=][1] _[ and there is at] least one fixed point in_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] . The messages converge to a fixed point ν[∗] of the generalized BP equations such that for any other fixed point µ, µi→jk ≤ νi[∗] →jk[.][Furthermore]_
comments.explanation: The proof uses monotonicity only on the restricted domain \([x^*,1)^n\). Indeed, a few lines later it shows the comparison only for fixed points \(\mu\in[x^*,1)^n\): “for any fixed point \(\mu \in [x^*,1)^n\)”. The theorem statement, however, says “for any other fixed point \(\mu\)” without that domain qualification. This matters because the map is not shown to be monotone outside the nonnegative/high-message region, and for higher-order odd interactions the update can behave differently when some incoming messages are negative. Unless the authors prove separately that all GBP fixed points lie in \([x^*,1)^n\), the theorem should restrict the comparison to fixed points in that domain.

comments.title: The final optimization argument needs an unproved coordinatewise ordering for arbitrary post-fixpoints
comments.quote: Finally, consider any other point _ν ∈ S_ post, by convexity of _S_ post, we know that the line segment from _ν_ to _ν[∗]_ is entirely contained in _S_ post. By Lemma 3, we see that for any point _x_ on this interpolating line segment that _∇G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _·_ ( _ν[∗] − ν_ ) _≥_ 0, and integrating over this line segment gives us _G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _≤ G_ Bethe( _ν[∗]_ ).
comments.explanation:
The structure of the argument is recognizable from monotone-map proofs: convexity of a post-fixpoint region plus a gradient sign condition can imply optimality. But the displayed reasoning has a missing step. Lemma 3 gives a coordinatewise sign for the gradient on \(S_{post}\); to conclude \(
abla G\cdot(
u^*-
u)\ge 0\), one also needs \(
u^*-
u\ge 0\) coordinatewise along the segment, or some equivalent directional-gradient statement. The proof has only established dominance of \(
u^*\) over fixed points in the restricted domain, not over every \(
u\in S_{post}\). It may be possible to prove this using monotone iteration/Tarski-style reasoning, depending on the exact definition of \(S_{post}\), but that step is not supplied here. As written, the inequality in the dot product does not follow merely from convexity of \(S_{post}\) and Lemma 3.

comments.title: The claimed global optimality is only shown on a restricted message region, not for the full Bethe free-energy problem
comments.quote: Based on Lemma 2 and 3, we can prove our main theorem.
comments.explanation: The surrounding paper frames the contribution as convergence to the global maximum/global optimum of the Bethe free energy. In this passage, however, the actual theorem assumes a fixed point in \([x^*,1)^n\), uses monotonicity only on that domain, and the final comparison is made for points in \(S_{post}\). That is a much more restricted statement than global optimality over all locally consistent beliefs, or even over all dual-message configurations/stationary points. Fixed points or stationary points outside the high-message nonnegative region are not ruled out, and neither is a larger Bethe value outside \(S_{post}\). The authors may intend a domain-restricted global maximum analogous to Koehler’s result, but the text around the theorem overstates what the proof actually establishes.

comments.title: Primal and dual Bethe free energies are conflated in the gradient argument
comments.quote: By Lemma 3, we see that for any point _x_ on this interpolating line segment that _∇G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _·_ ( _ν[∗] − ν_ ) _≥_ 0, and integrating over this line segment gives us _G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _≤ G_ Bethe( _ν[∗]_ ).
comments.explanation:
Earlier in the paper \(G_{\mathrm{Bethe}}(P)\) is the primal Bethe free energy as a function of beliefs, while \(G^*_{\mathrm{Bethe}}(
u)\) is the dual Bethe free energy as a function of messages. Lemma 3 is explicitly about \(
abla G^*_{\mathrm{Bethe}}(
u)\). In the quoted argument, the notation switches to \(
abla G_{\mathrm{Bethe}}(
u)\) and \(G_{\mathrm{Bethe}}(
u)\), which is not the previously defined object. At fixed points the primal and dual values may correspond, but the line-segment integration is over message space and therefore should use the dual objective. This is likely a notation slip rather than a conceptual flaw, but it can mislead readers about which function is being optimized and differentiated.

comments.title: Initialization at message value one lies outside the stated open message domain
comments.quote: _Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi_[(0)] _→jk_[=][1] _[ and there is at] least one fixed point in_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] .
comments.explanation: The all-one initialization is standard in ferromagnetic BP arguments, so the authors’ intent is clear. Still, the message parametrization \(\nu=\tanh(\lambda')\) naturally gives \(\nu\in(-1,1)\) for finite fields, and Lemma 2 is stated on \([x^*,1)^n\), which excludes the initial value \(1\). The first update may be well-defined if all couplings are finite, since terms involve \(\tanh^{-1}(\theta)\) with \(\theta<1\), but this boundary/limit interpretation is not stated. A short clarification that the initialization is understood as a limit from below, or that finite couplings make the first iterate enter \([x^*,1)^n\), would avoid a small domain inconsistency in applying the lemma from the first iteration onward.
